video_title: The Most Optimistic Take on WW3 | Pippa Malmgren I Global Macro | Ep. 103
gist: Geopolitical strategist Pippa Malmgren argues the world is already in WW3 but is being sequenced deliberately toward a 'superpower hug' rapprochement, because direct kinetic conflict is unsurvivable and warfare is migrating to drones and space. She frames a US regime change around digital-dollar rails, the death of the petrodollar, and a new-nuclear/AI abundance boom, while co-host Jim pushes back hard that populism and inequality will force higher cost of capital and derail the abundance thesis.

insights.claim: The conflicts in Ukraine, Taiwan, Venezuela, Iran and elsewhere are not independent events but a deliberately sequenced strategy aimed at engineering a peace deal in Ukraine, an accommodation on Taiwan, and a broad superpower rapprochement.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: If conflicts are one coordinated negotiation rather than separate flashpoints, markets pricing each geopolitical risk in isolation are mispricing the path toward de-escalation.
insights.quote: They try to assess Ukraine on its own merits and Taiwan on its own merits and Venezuela. But in fact, they're sequenced very carefully and they are related.

insights.claim: Direct kinetic confrontation between nuclear superpowers is effectively impossible because escalation is non-linear and unsurvivable, which structurally forces the major powers toward a negotiated 'hug' rather than war.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A base case of forced de-escalation implies tail geopolitical hedges may be overpriced and risk assets under-price an eventual peace dividend.
insights.quote: The options for a direct kinetic confrontation between the superpowers literally doesn't exist. Because we have so many nuclear weapons now.

insights.claim: Every historical near-nuclear standoff (Kennedy-Khrushchev, Nixon-Brezhnev, Reagan-Gorbachev) has resolved in a leaders' embrace rather than exchange, so the current path is either the end of the world or a hug with no smooth middle.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A binary 'catastrophe or reconciliation' frame argues against pricing prolonged middle-state war and favors positioning for eventual normalization.
insights.quote: This is why I'm saying this may not be a smooth path to the hug... but fundamentally, the other alternative is impossible.

insights.claim: Ending wars requires an explicit 'forgiveness' mechanism (lustration, truth-and-reconciliation) that Malmgren says markets have not priced, including Russia being restored to the world economy as part of a Ukraine deal.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A Russia reintegration scenario would reopen Russian commodities, assets and trade flows that are currently sanctioned and un-investable.
insights.quote: There's an element of forgiveness that has to happen and always happens at the end of wars... And I think in our markets, people have not clocked this.

insights.claim: Peace in Ukraine is being pursued via the US publicly acknowledging its own role in fomenting the conflict, with the administration raiding NGOs and having shut down USAID it alleges ran a parallel foreign policy.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A narrative shift toward US culpability signals genuine intent to strike a Ukraine deal, a catalyst for European defense, energy and reconstruction plays.

insights.claim: Cutting off Venezuelan and Iranian oil was a lever to pressure China: Beijing can buy that oil freely unless it wants war with the US, which controls the spigot, pushing China toward alignment against Russia.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Oil-supply coercion as a China-alignment tool ties Venezuela/Iran actions directly to US-China detente odds and to global crude flow expectations.
insights.quote: China can buy all the oil it wants from Venezuela. We would never get in the way of that, of course, unless you want to go to war with the United States.

insights.claim: The US and China are now aligned on two principles: no nuclear escalation in Ukraine, and keeping global oil markets flowing, with the US offering to sell China Alaskan oil while Iran redirects its oil to China.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: US-China oil alignment reshuffles crude trade routes (Alaska to China, Iran to China) and isolates Middle East disruptors, a watch item for tanker and crude spreads.

insights.claim: The Trump administration treats Taiwan as expendable strategic real estate, moving semiconductor production and personnel from Taiwan to Texas and Arizona and shifting the front-line tripwire role to a rearming Japan.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: US onshoring of chip fabs plus a Taiwan accommodation reduces Taiwan Strait tail risk and favors US-based semiconductor capacity over Taiwan-concentrated supply.
insights.quote: Now we're moving the semiconductor production and the personnel and the machines from Taiwan to Texas and Arizona. And therefore, this ceases to have the strategic importance.

insights.claim: Malmgren cites the positioning of US special forces on Kinmen island and the post-Trump-Xi announcement of tourism across the strait as subtle signals of quiet diplomacy normalizing the Taiwan relationship.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Reading obscure signals like Kinmen tourism as de-escalation markers offers a leading indicator for cross-strait risk ahead of headline news.

insights.claim: The US offer to China is to help it move from cheap exports to owning global brands (mirroring postwar Japan and Germany), including a Trump-Xi board reviewing which Chinese firms can enter US markets on favorable terms.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A pivot from tariff-cancellation to Chinese-brand integration would be a regime change for China trade exposure and specific consumer/tech names entering the US.
insights.quote: It will be stamped owned by Japan, owned by Germany. Because now you're talking about building global brands.

insights.claim: Malmgren argues China's economy is dangerously brittle from tight government control and 'keeps glitching,' with the commercially-minded Shanghai branch of the Communist Party favoring more market forces.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A leadership split over liberalization is a signal to watch for China policy easing versus continued state control that would cap Chinese asset performance.

themes.theme: War moves to space, drones and 'bloodless' robot warfare

insights.claim: Warfare is migrating from land to space, so even the Ukraine war is fundamentally a space war dependent on mega-constellations of satellites for offensive and defensive operations.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Framing conflict as space-based elevates satellite constellations, launch and space-defense assets as the strategic 'commanding heights' for investment.
insights.quote: Land wars are now space wars. Ukraine is a space war. People think it's a land war because we see the images of tanks on the ground.

insights.claim: Only the US and China currently have satellites with robotic arms that can grab another satellite and hurl it into deep space, and Russia has destroyed its own aging satellites to create debris fields.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Demonstrated orbital counter-space capability signals an arms race in space defense and anti-satellite tech worth tracking among defense primes.
insights.quote: The Chinese demonstrate that they have arms on their satellites and they can go up to a satellite, grab it, and literally throw it into outer space.

insights.claim: Future drones will be AI-driven and able to detect and kill a human before he hears them, forcing humans off the battlefield entirely and ushering in 'bloodless' robot-on-robot warfare.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A shift to autonomous robot warfare reprices defense toward AI, autonomy and drone-swarm makers over legacy manned platforms.
insights.quote: The drone will be AI-driven and therefore it will detect your heart. It will kill you before you even hear it. So, now we have to remove humans from the battlefield altogether.

insights.claim: Ukraine is currently the only actor with enough drones to sustain a kinetic conflict, making drone mass a decisive and scarce military capability.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Drone-production capacity as the binding military constraint highlights Ukraine's manufacturing base and drone supply chains as strategic assets.

themes.theme: Dollar regime change and death of the petrodollar

insights.claim: Treasury Secretary Bessent is pursuing a move of the US financial system onto digital rails to make the economy visible and transparent and to trace where all the money goes.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A push toward a fully digital/traceable dollar system has major implications for payments infrastructure, stablecoins and financial privacy plays.
insights.quote: Secretary of the Treasury Bessent has a very clear vision of moving the nation from its current financial system onto digital rails.

insights.claim: Malmgren declares the petrodollar is dead and replaced by a system where money flows into the US because it leads in innovation and technology, specifically power generation, rather than oil settlement.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: If dollar demand is anchored to US tech/energy leadership rather than oil, it reframes the dollar's reserve status around innovation dominance and capital inflows into US tech.
insights.quote: We don't have a petrodollar anymore. The petrodollar is done.

insights.claim: Jim argues the US is leaning into the 'exorbitant privilege' of the dollar as asymmetric leverage, using it to potentially monetize the national debt, backstop the too-big-to-fail equity market, and buy off populism by putting people into equities.
insights.speaker: Jim (co-host)
insights.so_what: A deliberate dollar-printing strategy to resolve debt and prop equities implies structural support for US stocks alongside currency debasement risk.
insights.quote: If you can lean on that and print dollars, much like Japan did, right? We can resolve the national debt. We can support the two big to fail equity market.

themes.theme: New nuclear, fusion and the abundance thesis

insights.claim: The US is deregulating small modular reactors ('a star in a box'), roughly car-sized and mobile, to deliver near-unlimited energy anywhere, which Malmgren says markets have not priced.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A near-term SMR rollout would be a step-change for power supply, data-center energy and nuclear-linked equities the market is under-appreciating.

insights.claim: Malmgren names Valor Atomics in California and Oklo in Austin as new-nuclear companies she is watching, saying investors have not understood how transformational the sector is.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Specific named new-nuclear companies are direct watchlist candidates for the SMR/abundance theme.
insights.quote: I'm watching companies like Valor Atomics in California. I'm watching um Oklo in Austin.

insights.claim: An Austin company built a small modular reactor with fewer than 300 engineers in under 365 days, collapsing the traditional nuclear timeline and cost assumption of ~30 years.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A 300-people/365-day build radically changes nuclear project economics, supporting a faster, cheaper capacity buildout than consensus models assume.
insights.quote: They've built a small modular reactor with fewer than 300 engineers in less than 365 days.

insights.claim: Malmgren believes nuclear fusion may already exist or be very close, arguing AI running billions of scenarios per minute plus atom-by-atom new materials for containment have compressed the traditional '50 years away' timeline.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A fusion breakthrough would be a paradigm shift for energy markets; even the possibility argues for optionality on fusion and enabling-materials plays.
insights.quote: There's a big discussion about do we already have it in the national labs? They haven't been willing to release it cuz they see it as a national security advantage.

insights.claim: The White House 'Genesis mission' aims to open classified national-lab data to AI to connect discoveries and move them from behind the national-security wall into the public domain.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Declassifying lab research to AI could accelerate commercialization of energy and materials breakthroughs, a catalyst for the abundance-tech complex.

insights.claim: Cheap, abundant new-nuclear power resolves the populist backlash against data centers by removing the energy/water trade-off, so the binding question becomes how fast reactors can come online.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: SMR deployment speed is the gating variable for the AI data-center buildout and its social license, a key watch item for AI-infrastructure timelines.

themes.theme: Populism vs. capital: the inequality counter-thesis

insights.claim: Jim argues the drive for fairness in an era of extreme populism has historically always pushed cost of capital higher, which slows capital flowing to technology and derails supply-side abundance booms.
insights.speaker: Jim (co-host)
insights.so_what: If populism forces a higher cost of capital, it is a structural headwind to the tech/abundance boom and argues for a regime of higher rates and inflation.
insights.quote: That drive for more fairness inevitably, throughout history, has always... driven to higher cost of capital. And a higher cost of capital drives money away from capital and technological development.

insights.claim: Jim contends AI's gains, if left untouched, will accrue to a very small cohort of owners, and the eventual redistribution of that wealth to people will be incredibly inflationary.
insights.speaker: Jim (co-host)
insights.so_what: A thesis that AI wealth concentration ends in inflationary redistribution supports long-inflation and hard-asset positioning against a disinflation-forever view.
insights.quote: The redistribution of that wealth will be incredibly inflationary.

insights.claim: US median real income growth has averaged just 0.75% per year for 40 years because manufacturing was offshored and labor increasingly competes with technology, driving the K-shaped economy.
insights.speaker: Jim (co-host)
insights.so_what: A 0.75%/yr real median income figure quantifies the inequality fueling populism and the political risk to capital-friendly policy.
insights.quote: In the last 40 years, median income growth is 0.75% per year in real terms for 40 years.

insights.claim: Jim warns of concrete political risk to AI ownership: Gavin Newsom's recent moves and Bernie Sanders proposing the government take 50% ownership of all AI businesses, positions he says poll as very popular.
insights.speaker: Jim (co-host)
insights.so_what: Popular proposals to seize AI-business ownership are a tail policy risk for mega-cap AI valuations heading into the 2028/2032 election cycles.
insights.quote: He's talking about taking 50% ownership of all AI businesses.

insights.claim: Jim characterizes the last 40 years of supply-side economics as 'sending money to planet Palo Alto,' yielding cheaper technology but ever-greater inequality rather than shared prosperity.
insights.speaker: Jim (co-host)
insights.so_what: Reframing 'more money equals deflation' as the very engine of inequality challenges the abundance-deflation narrative underpinning bullish tech positioning.
insights.quote: We've been sending money to planet Palo Alto for 40 years, and all we get back are cheaper technologies and goods that ultimately cause greater and greater inequality.

insights.claim: Malmgren counters that Kevin Warsh's 'regime change' inverts the old monetary formula: with new technologies, more money directed to innovation collapses the cost of energy, labor and materials, so more money now equals deflation, not inflation.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: If a Warsh-led Fed adopts a 'more money equals deflation' framework, it could justify easier policy despite fiscal expansion, reshaping rate expectations.
insights.quote: Their view is now the formula reads more money in the system equals deflation.

insights.claim: Malmgren argues massive computational power plus AI breaks the old 'more capital equals more technology' formula, letting builders create advanced things (like SMRs) with far less money and fewer people.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: If AI collapses the capital intensity of hard-tech, high rates matter less for innovation pace, undercutting the bear case that expensive capital stalls the boom.
insights.quote: The combination of vastly more computational power and artificial intelligence means you can build amazing things with very little money.

insights.claim: Malmgren cites South Korea and Sweden, the two countries with the highest robotics and AI implementation, as having generated jobs since automation began, disputing the claim that AI/robotics net-destroys employment.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Evidence that high-automation economies added jobs supports a productivity-not-unemployment view of AI, informing labor and equity assumptions.
insights.quote: The two countries with the highest level of implementation of robotics and AI, South Korea and Sweden have generated jobs since this process began.

themes.theme: Greenland, the Arctic and space access

insights.claim: Greenland's sudden strategic importance is driven by space: polar satellites are most efficient in the Arctic Circle and there is a premium on space data links routed through the Arctic.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Tying Greenland/Arctic geopolitics to satellite data access reframes the Greenland dispute as a space-infrastructure land grab, relevant to space and defense themes.
insights.quote: There's a premium on access to space via data links through the Arctic.

insights.claim: The US wants a sovereign Arctic base on Greenland to watch the GIUK gap, anticipating that a failure to resolve Ukraine will push Russia to move nuclear submarines and ships into the Atlantic through the Greenland-Iceland-UK gap.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Linking Greenland basing to the Ukraine outcome makes Arctic militarization a barometer for whether the Ukraine war de-escalates or escalates into the Atlantic.

insights.claim: Malmgren says the real friction over Greenland is between the US and Denmark, not the Greenlanders, noting Denmark had tried to offload expensive Greenland for years before reversing when the US engaged.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Framing Greenland as a US-Denmark dispute suggests intra-NATO tension is a harder rapprochement than the superpower one, a risk to European cohesion.

themes.theme: Non-human intelligence, AGI and reality's aperture

insights.claim: Malmgren argues we may be at an inflection akin to the discovery of X-rays, where new tools widen our perceptual aperture and reveal 'non-human intelligence' that was previously dismissed as imagination.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A framework treating NHI/UAP as a perceptual-tool revolution rather than fringe topic signals a coming shift in what governments will disclose and fund.

insights.claim: US government UAP/non-human-intelligence declassification has drawn over a billion unique visitors, the single most popular topic ever for the US government, with ~85% of both Democrats and Republicans wanting more transparency.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Rare bipartisan 85% support and record public engagement suggests disclosure is a politically safe, accelerating trend worth tracking for its market and tech spillovers.
insights.quote: They've had over a billion unique visitors, making it the single most popular topic ever for the US government on any subject.

insights.claim: As AI approaches AGI, Malmgren says the line between artificial intelligence and non-human intelligence blurs into one of the most profound questions of the era, with AGI 'about to be something there.'
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: Positioning AGI as imminent and category-blurring reinforces the case that AI is the dominant strategic and investment axis of the decade.

insights.claim: Malmgren takes the contrarian view against the 'AI will kill us all' consensus, arguing AI is more likely the key to extending lifespan, raising living standards and potentially disintermediating war itself.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: An explicitly optimistic AI thesis against doomer consensus supports staying long the AI-abundance complex rather than hedging existential risk.
insights.quote: The standard view right now is AI is going to kill us all. And I, as you know, take the opposite view.

insights.claim: Malmgren's late father, an advisor to multiple presidents, said Putin is the only head of state he ever met who spent nearly three years studying nuclear strategy and deeply understands it, which she uses to argue Putin will not press the nuclear button.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: An argument that Putin's nuclear expertise makes escalation less likely supports discounting the extreme nuclear-use tail in Ukraine risk pricing.
insights.quote: Putin is the only head of state he's ever met who spent nearly 3 years studying nuclear strategy.

insights.claim: Malmgren argues the Cold War was won not by weapons but by culture, rock and roll, blue jeans and the Beatles, contending cultural contact beats military confrontation as a tool of influence.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A soft-power-wins framework favors reading cultural and economic engagement, not troop movements, as the leading indicator of geopolitical outcomes.
insights.quote: Cold War... was not won with bullets and missiles. It was won with rock and roll and blue jeans.

insights.claim: Malmgren stresses that nations are not monoliths and effective foreign policy hinges on human-to-human trust and accommodating a multiplicity of views within each country.
insights.speaker: Pippa Malmgren
insights.so_what: A reminder to distinguish factions within states (e.g., Shanghai Communist Party) helps investors read policy fights rather than treat 'China' or 'Russia' as single actors.
